game_name: Double Dragon III: The Sacred Stones

danger_signals.visual_form: Onscreen enemy sprites with distinct character models moving toward player
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring combat engagement or evasion

danger_signals.visual_form: Player character sprite damage state or HUD health bar reduction
danger_signals.meaning: Accumulating damage from enemy attacks; risk of knockout if depleted

danger_signals.visual_form: Limited lateral movement space, enemies approaching from both sides
danger_signals.meaning: High-pressure combat scenario with limited escape routes

safety_signals.visual_form: Stage transition screen displaying purchasable items (Energy, Power Up, Weapons, Extra Guys)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe preparation period before combat; opportunity to increase survivability

safety_signals.visual_form: Character swap mechanic; visual replacement of active fighter with reserve fighter
safety_signals.meaning: Health reset and tactical advantage through character rotation

safety_signals.visual_form: Final enemy defeated; transition to next mission screen

faction_indicators.color_identity: Blue and Red (traditional series color differentiation)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Starting position at stage entrance; player-controlled characters

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Mixed martial arts champions; alternate playable team
Tai chi experts; specialist fighting style
Palette swap of Lee brothers; third player character
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Three-player cabinet exclusive; integrated into player team

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stage introduction screen showing geographic location (Egypt, etc.); enemy sprite patterns distributed across stage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies spawn in waves or patrol positions; players positioned at stage left entrance

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple enemy sprites visible onscreen; collision detection active; visual feedback from hit interactions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies attack with directional movement; players execute punch/kick/special move animations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Health bar near depletion or character sprite shows damage state (visual wear/damage texture)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Character vulnerability increases; knockback effect more pronounced; risk of stage restart

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Final boss or enemy wave defeated; stage clear transition animation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies cease spawning; progression to next mission stage unlocked

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Mission structure changes (Mission 1-5); environmental theming shifts (Egypt setting implied through background art)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Different enemy rosters per mission; weapon availability varies by mission (nunchaku in Mission 2, sword in Missions 3 and 5)

environmental_storytelling.example: Urquidez brothers only purchasable/selectable in Missions 1 and 5; implies geographic or narrative availability tied to specific locations in worldwide journey
Nunchaku exclusive to Mission 2; sword appears in Missions 3 and 5; implies regional combat traditions or enemy equipment types
Multiple brother-team fighters (Lee, Urquidez, Chin, Ōyama) suggest worldwide martial arts tournament or gathering; cultural representation through fighting style specialist teams
Item shops appear at mission start before combat; visual framing suggests safe harbor or preparation zone before entering hostile territory

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Moves toward player using joystick directional input; initiates attack animations (punch, kick, or martial arts-specific technique)
Can execute standard punch/kick, special moves (solo hurricane kick, jumping throw, running head-butt), coordinate multi-player moves (back-to-back hurricane kick, triangle jump kick)
Execute martial art style-specific techniques; cannot use purchasable weapons (Urquidez, Chin, Ōyama exclusion); inherit special move purchases from previous character
Knockout animation; if extra fighters available, automatic character swap to next reserve fighter; if no reserves, stage restart
Displays purchasable items; costs one credit per item; player can invest in Energy, Power Up, Tricks, Weapons, or Extra Guys

notes: Double Dragon III employs a progression-based visual communication system where mission-gated character availability and weapon types directly signal player capability expansion and world narrative (worldwide journey to collect Rosetta Stones). The item shop mechanic creates distinct 'safe preparation' vs 'active threat' visual states. Faction identity is communicated primarily through character team composition (sibling groups with martial art specialization) rather than visual colors or environmental markers. The NES version inherits these systems from the arcade original but adapts character selection mechanics.
